source: Gaze Glass — A Family of Mortals (2).docx
heading: 4 Rashid
paragraphs: Marok loathed being in The Realm of the Dead. He lazed on a cloud and shivered when icy wind met his eyes. His ears perked up and tail flew between his legs when he remembered his master’s words. I will make an example of him if he fails his first assignment.

A mile below, Rashid and Jermaine argued, sweat rolling down their faces. The calf strayed away from the boys and bobbed its head up and down as it approached a rusty cart flowering with long, overripe bananas. The merchant lit a stick of jasmine incense before he noticed and raised a thin metal pole above his head. Rashid, knowing very well that some merchants were cruel, lunged forward and wrapped his arm around the hungry calf. He used all of his strength to retain his new friend. “I’d like to buy some bananas for my cow!” Rashid said anxiously in Hindi.

The merchant rested his grip on the pole and grunted. “Could I have this one?” Rashid asked, pointing to the banana the calf was trying to reach with its long, pink tongue. “This is the finest fruit I’ve seen!” Jermaine crouched next to his best friend and grinned so wide you couldn’t see his eyes.
“By weight,” the merchant responded coldly, pointing to a metal scale with two arms.
Sweet jasmine incense swirled around the boys as they watched the merchant hobble to the side of the cart. He used a shepherd’s hook to lift a stalk of bananas from the cart. “How much do you want?”
“1 kg,” Rashid replied. “Please.” A black crow landed on the left scale and raised its silky wings. But it ascended when the merchant turned to face it, forcing the scales to dance.
“They get hungry, too,” Rashid said in English.
“What?” the merchant asked in Hindi.

After a moment of silence, the boys petted the calf; the merchant played with the scale: bananas were added one by one on the right arm, small metal squares—labeled by weight—were carefully added on the left arm, and everyone watched the arms rise and fall. After a few seconds, the arm with the bananas was raised four inches higher than the left arm. “What is he doing?” Jermaine asked.

“It’s all about balance. One arm can’t be higher or lower than the other. When they are perfectly even, the weight of the bananas are calculated,” Rashid explained. “He’s going to add weights until then.”
Jermaine nodded. “Yo. We should grab it and make a run for it.”
“His hanging scales?” Rashid asked. “You’ll never get away with it.”
“Only time will tell,” Jermaine said, winking. “Why don’t they use digital scales?”
Rashid scratched behind the cow’s ear. “Bro. They don’t even have phones here.”
The merchant added two smaller green bananas to the scale and squinted.

“1 kg — three hundred rupees,“ the merchant said, eyes fixed on Jermaine. Rashid straightened his spine and retrieved a stack of silver coins from his pocket. “Dammit!” Rashid fussed. “I forgot to haggle.”

“Yeah, man. Didn’t we pay thirty-five rupees at the cart near the cottage?” Jermaine asked. “You want me to break his scale?” Rashid groaned. “And how are you planning to do that?” Jermaine grinned. “I’ll run up the highest mountain and throw it in the river!”
“Your big plan is to let his hanging scales rust?” Rashid asked.
“We can’t break the thing with our hands. We gotta let time do its thing!”
Rashid let out a long sigh and watched the merchant bag the fruit in a burlap sack.
“Want. One. Cow. Eat. Now!” Jermaine said in Hindi, fumbling each word.
The merchant arched an eyebrow and shifted his gaze to Rashid. Jermaine’s face brightened when the merchant handed him a banana. “See. Your Hindi is getting better!” Rashid cheered.

“Then why did he look at me like that?” Jermaine asked. The calf watched Jermaine’s hand lower and flicked its long pink tongue; it grabbed the banana with haste. Jermaine cringed, retracting his hand, and shuddered. He wiped the cow’s thick saliva from his fingers on Rashid’s arm. The boys cringed and laughed. As they ventured through the town, Rashid became Jermaine’s tour guide.

The giddy calf cooperated until it saw a crone with a face full of sunspots in front of a blue building. Her long, white hair looked as if it were made of spun cotton. The black dress that covered her arms and legs sparkled like little stars against her golden brown skin when she moved.
“Chotu!” the woman shouted upon seeing the calf.
The cow ran to her and the boys followed in a panic.
“How are you?” Jermaine asked in Hindi.
“You are getting better,” the woman replied in English.
“That’s what I said!” Rashid exclaimed.
The woman lowered herself into a squat. And the cow licked her chin.
“Grandma—what is this place?” Rashid asked in English.

Grandmother cleared her throat and arched a brow. Rashid gasped and folded to touch his grandmother’s bare feet. Jermaine’s jaw dropped. He did the same. “Shit! Sorry!” Jermaine said. “Sorry for saying ‘shit’ and I’m sorry about that, too.” Locals watched Jermaine lower himself to the ground to show the old woman respect. And they whispered. Unfortunately, not all of the words were nice.
What is that foreigner doing?
He doesn’t belong here!
He must be a servant.
Rashid’s grandmother knelt and scooped Jermaine’s soft face into her worn hands.
“You don’t have to do that,” she said warmly.
Nearby whispers became audible conversations. Gazes hardened to bullets.
“Why are they like this?” Rashid asked as he looked at the scowling and sneering locals.
Grandmother let go of Jermaine’s face and turned her head in every direction.
“Pay them no attention. They will be judged one day for their wickedness.”
“I’d like to see that happen!” Rashid exclaimed, tilting his head up. “What is this place?”

Grandmother’s hazel eyes shifted to Jermaine. “Go down to the chemist and get a bottle of soda water. Get some chocolates for you and Rashid.” Jermaine refused the money she offered him and bolted down the dirt trail, coins jingling in his pockets.
“Is your stomach upset? Why the soda?” Rashid asked his grandmother.
“It’s for your friend. He looks constipated.”
“I think he’s emotionally constipated, too.”  Rashid smiled.

The calf lowered its head to the pavement and searched for food. Grandmother turned her chin over her right shoulder and looked at the abandoned building made of blue slabs of stone. The entryway was barred with mismatching panels of wood and the grimy roof cast a shadow on the ground. On each side of the roof, intricate rows of square holes devoured sunlight. The old stone beams of the monument had subtle geometric shapes engraved in a repeating pattern—and the tall grass around the building had grown high and become as dry as summer soil. “My mother told stories of an evil witch. She lived here. The woman was once an honorable priestess—and served the royal family,” Grandmother said.
“Why was she evil?” Rashid asked. “What did she do?”
Grandmother licked her lips. “Some say the royal family took her in after discovering her gifts. Despite their generosity, she betrayed them—brutally murdered them when they needed her help.”

Rashid stepped back and squinted at the building. The dusty, black obsidian steps leading to the building bore deep cracks. From them, dead plants stood and resisted the wind. Seven rusted locks ran down the left side of the bolted door.
“Maybe she tried to abolish the monarchy,” Rashid said. “I would.”
“Why do you say that?”
“No group of humans should hold so much power over others. No billionaires.”

“What is that?” Grandmother asked in Hindi. “Very selfish people who believe they are worth more than others,” Rashid said, frowning. Grandmother wove her plump fingers through Rashid’s twiggy fingers and pulled him toward the black steps. The wind picked up and brushed against Rashid’s bruised ankles. When Rashid placed his right foot on the first step, his white shoe withered until all that was left were leather straps around his feet. “No—please!” Rashid begged. Grandmother caressed his hand with her thumb and grinned. “Embrace your power!”

Rashid swallowed the wad of spit that collected in the center of his throat and felt it wash away the knot in his stomach. When his left foot fell onto the first step, the wooden panels on the doorway transformed into sand and fell to the floor. Then scattered away, leaving behind a tall archway made of polished, white limestone. Deep within the darkness, bright lights flickered—and the scent of sandalwood and frankincense took Rashid far from Rishikesh. “This is a temple—” Rashid started to say. Grandmother let go of his hands. When she did, the lights faded; the limestone archway turned back into unpolished stone, and the doorway was covered with wooden panels once again.
“What’s the point of having a gift if I can’t control it?” Rashid muttered.
“Let go of control and trust yourself.”
“I don’t even like myself.”
Rashid turned to face his grandmother, but she had already reached the last step and greeted Jermaine as he appeared with several plastic bags. Rashid closed his eyes and sighed.
“Do you know who this calf belongs to?” Jermaine asked.
“Chotu lives by the—uh—near the cremation grounds,” Grandmother replied. “There’s a red cottage down the road, past an apple tree, near the sunflower field.”
“Are you talking about the cottage near dad’s mosque?” Rashid asked in Hindi.
Jermaine cleared his throat, but Rashid ignored him.

“Yes. If you leave now, you can make it there before dark,” Grandmother said in Hindi. “Rashid. One more thing. Do not go anywhere near that mosque. The building is not stable. Do you understand?” Rashid nodded. “I do.”

Jermaine cleared his throat twice to get Rashid’s attention. “Your mom told us to find your brother and bring him back before sunset.” And he was right, but Rashid felt wrong about abandoning the calf; he chewed his lower lip. Thunder rolled across the sleepy town as a light mist rolled in. The purple sky darkened to crimson as locals rushed the market. “Have you seen Sahil?” Rashid asked in English.
“He’s at his first scripture lesson,” Grandmother replied. “Isn’t he?”
Rashid grimaced. “No. He hasn’t been home all day. Mom only lets him go outside for an hour. He’s not supposed to leave the village. I’m worried. I think something might have happened to him.”
“Has he ever left the village on his own?” Grandmother asked.

Rashid and Jermaine faced each other and sighed in unison. The boys opened their mouths at the same time, but they weren’t able to get a word out. Grandmother groaned and walked briskly toward the banana merchant. At first, Rashid could hear fragments of the conversation until a chill ran down his spine. Quickly, he turned around to face the old building only to discover it wasn’t old anymore. A black cobra emerged from a diamond-shaped hole on the roof and slithered up the building. “Grandmother—” Rashid called out in Hindi.

Every sound began to fade: the conversations, feet scuttling against the dirt road, and the bells of a nearby temple. Rashid’s vision blurred and he took a few steps toward the limestone archway. But Jermaine’s voice broke the trance before he could go further. The building had returned to ruins and Rashid exhaled the fear that had welled up in his chest. “Hey! Let’s go find your brother, return the cow, and get back to the cottage. We can’t be out late. We have yoga class and then—“ Jermaine started to say.
“I know! Before we go to the sacred caves. I swear. You talk about it more than my parents!”
Jermaine shivered and wiped a necklace made of sweat away from his neck.
“Because this is really important for your family,” Jermaine said.
“For my parents,” Rashid muttered. “I wish I could move out already.”
“At least you still have parents,” Jermaine replied.
“Why does it always have to be a competition?” Rashid asked.
“It’s not. Look. The sacred caves are—”
“Why are you so obsessed with—?” Rashid started to say with angry eyes.
“I’m not. All I’m saying is your family takes this pilgrimage seriously!”
Just then, Rashid saw the tea merchant cross the road ahead. She waved at him and smiled.
“How serious?” Rashid asked with a devilish smile.
Jermaine followed Rashid’s gaze and waved back. “Like, when Sonam—”

Rashid let out a roar of laughter, attracting the attention of two fluffy, gray monkeys sitting on a twisty branch of a tall mango tree. “I’m sorry. I’m sorry. I still can’t get over that time she accidentally got high and tried to hide it from my mom. She’s such a bad actress!” Tears of joy rolled down his cheeks. “I seriously wish she wasn’t such a witch. I can’t stand her.”
Sunlight left Rashid’s face. Jermaine held his breath. “I think you’re the first black dude who has ever been in this village,” Rashid said and sighed before placing his hands on his hips.
“I’m used to it.” Jermaine shrugged. “But you know what? I love learning more about where you’re from. This place is wild, but it’s your home. I’ll miss you.”

The boys had graduated from Brooklyn Academy a few weeks ago. Saddened that they would be heading to different colleges, Rashid was excited to be Jermaine’s tour guide in India. And Jermaine was eager to visit the sacred caves—a series of underground passages that ran through an ancient city along the banks of the Ganges, a sacred river where devotees prayed to the gods to break free from the cycle of reincarnation. ”What was it like growing up here? What’s life like without prejudice?” Jermaine asked.
Rashid bit his lower lip and searched his mind for the right words. “Racism isn’t a thing here unless people see tourists from other countries. But we do have a caste system.”
“What’s that?”
“Sonam could explain it way better than I could.”
“You’re just as smart as her.”

Rashid licked his lips and squinted. “Basically, ancient India had this whole idea that people were reincarnated into specific social classes. The priests are at the very top. They ruled. After that, you have the warriors. Then the merchants and anyone who owns land. Below that, you have commoners and maids,” Rashid explained. “And there’s the people who aren’t good enough to be in the system. They clean toilets and are given jobs that are considered too awful for a normal person to do.”
Jermaine blew out his cheeks. “Yeah. I’d rather deal with high school than be in that world.”

“Luckily, my family used to own a business here. After that, they built a few holy places since there weren’t many at the time. You know how my dad is Muslim? He built a mosque in case any of us wanted to explore his faith. He believed we should be able to choose our own path. The locals burned it down,” Rashid admitted gloomily.
“Why?” Jermaine inquired. “Why would they do that?”

“That’s not the worst part,” Rashid said. “My parents tried getting the police involved, but they refused to take action. My mom thinks they were in on it.” Jermaine squinted. “But aren’t all Indians the same?”

“Different parts of India have different languages and beliefs. My family is from the south. We speak Tamil. Those dialects aren’t common in the north. We’re from different castes and regions.” Jermaine raised a brow and pulled his lips between his teeth. “Remember when you, Kevante, Felix, Tamara, and I went to that restaurant in Tribeca for breakfast?” Rashid asked. “The people who worked there weren’t nice to us, but they were nice to the white kids at the table next to us.”
Jermaine nodded. “Yeah. I remember. I wanted to slap them with my waffle.”
Rashid muffled a chuckle with his hand. “My family received a lot of silence from the people here after they opened a business. We’ve felt invisible ever since. So, we built the temples.”
”I’ve felt that way my whole life,” Jermaine admitted.

Before Jermaine could say another word, a black viper lunged across a tree branch above and sank its fangs into a baby crow. They fell onto the ground in front of the boys. Rashid maneuvered the calf away from the brawl. Jermaine—stricken by fear—stepped back quickly, lost balance, and fell to the ground. Rashid spared no time. He lifted him to his feet and pulled him away from the horrific scene. They walked for ten minutes without exchanging a word until Jermaine broke the silence.
“India is nothing like America,” Jermaine said.
“Told you,” Rashid nodded. “Let’s sit down for a minute.”

Rashid looked up at the banyan tree towering over his best friend, bearing vines like human veins. Mischievous monkeys leaped between branches, lurching them through the humid air as they scoured for bugs. Jermaine pressed his lips together and pulled out a little metal tin from his pocket. He opened the lid and picked up a yellow piece of candy. “These fruit drops are like crack.”  Jermaine popped the candy into his mouth and smiled. “Why do people even say that?” Rashid asked. “Crack destroys lives.”  Jermaine laughed. “I don’t know, but I would have a mental breakdown if I didn’t have these. They are keeping me from losing my mind.”
“I’ve noticed that you pop one into your mouth when you get stressed.”
Jermaine smiled before looking away. “When do we get to wear cool outfits like that?”
He nodded toward several men shuffling past in saffron robes. Rashid waited until they were further away.  Rolling his eyes, he whispered, “They’re monks. I don't think either of us would fit in.”
It didn’t take long for Jermaine to think of another question. Butterflies flitted around the calf’s neck. “If he’s so hungry, why doesn’t he eat the butterflies?”
The calf searched the ground for food. “I don’t know,” Rashid responded. “Maybe he knows it’s wrong.”
“I wish humans did,” Jermaine said. “Knew right from wrong.”

“Same,” Rashid admitted. “I wish people were nicer to each other.” 
     Rashid heaved a dry cough into the humid air. The stench of cow manure was attracting swarms of flies. Rashid swatted his sweaty neck with one hand and fumbled around the inside of his linen pants for a vial—a tincture his grandmother made to alleviate his asthma. “Wh-what is that?” Jermaine asked, covering his nose and mouth with his sweaty forearm. Rashid retrieved the vial and held it out to Jermaine. “Don’t be stupid! You’re the one with bad lungs.”

The calf wrinkled its cracked, black nose and squinted its black, beady eyes. Rashid focused on them, and their thick lashes while he coughed. Suddenly, a monkey jumped from the branches above them and landed on Jermaine’s head. He screamed and, without thinking, Rashid hurled the vial at the monkey. It worked. The monkey scurried back up the tree’s thick trunk and the boys stood in shaken silence for a moment, until Jermaine broke it with a shout: “I HATE MONKEYS!”

Rashid laughed. But Jermaine didn’t. He turned and began walking again, then stopped as suddenly as he had started. “Oh no! Bro! He was literally right here. Where did he go?” Jermaine asked, noticing somewhere within the commotion, the calf had disappeared.
